title: Literary Devices
subject: English
objectives: Identify common literary devices in texts
Understand the purpose of figurative language

sections.heading: What are literary devices?
sections.content: Literary devices are techniques that writers use to convey meaning, create effects, and engage readers beyond the literal meaning of words. They are the tools of a writer's craft, used to create vivid imagery, build emotion, and add layers of meaning to a text.

sections.heading: Key devices to know
sections.content:
• Simile: a comparison using 'like' or 'as.' 'Her smile was like sunshine.'
• Metaphor: a direct comparison without 'like' or 'as.' 'Time is money.'
• Personification: giving human qualities to non-human things. 'The wind whispered through the trees.'
• Hyperbole: exaggeration for emphasis. 'I've told you a million times.'
• Alliteration: repetition of initial consonant sounds. 'Peter Piper picked a peck.'
• Imagery: descriptive language appealing to the senses. 'The warm, golden bread crackled as she sliced through the crisp crust.'
• Symbolism: using an object to represent something abstract. A dove symbolizing peace.
• Irony: a contrast between expectation and reality. A fire station burning down.

sections.heading: Analyzing literary devices
sections.content:
Consider this passage from Romeo and Juliet:
'But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.'

Shakespeare uses a metaphor — comparing Juliet to the sun. This isn't literal; he means Juliet is radiant, life-giving, and the center of Romeo's world. The metaphor reveals the depth of Romeo's admiration more powerfully than simply saying 'Juliet is beautiful.'

This is why literary devices matter: they compress complex emotions and ideas into vivid, memorable language.
Literary devices are tools writers use to convey meaning beyond the literal. Recognizing them helps you understand deeper layers of any text and makes your own writing more powerful.

aiContext: This lesson covers literary devices at a high school English level. Students learn to identify simile, metaphor, personification, hyperbole, alliteration, imagery, symbolism, and irony, and analyze how they enhance meaning.
